nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rocky littoral zone; endemic to Boadzulu, Chinyankwazi, and Chinyamwezi Islands in the southeastern arm of Lake Malawi. Inhabits surge-washed boulder fields and rocky reefs in clear, hard, alkaline water.

decorPreferences: stacked rockwork with caves and crevices for territory and refuge
open sandy floor areas for natural foraging behaviour
minimal or no plants (not a planted-tank species)
smooth substrate (fine sand or fine gravel) to protect ventral surfaces

diet.notes: Omnivore with a herbivorous lean; the diet foundation should be high-quality spirulina flakes, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini). Small live and frozen foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops) can be offered as supplements. Bloodworms and other high-protein meaty foods should be fed only sparingly — excess protein is associated with Malawi bloat in this species. In the wild the fish grazes on aufwuchs (algae, diatoms, and associated micro-invertebrates) scraped from rocky surfaces.

goodWith: Aulonocara (peacock cichlids) — similar water needs, less competitive
Copadichromis and other open-water Malawi haplochromines
other mild mbuna of similar size (e.g., Cynotilapia afra)
avoidWith: large aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Pseudotropheus socolofi) — will outcompete and bully
Petrotilapia and other dominant rock-territory mbuna
small peaceful community fish — mbuna water chemistry incompatible
any fish unable to hold its own in a Malawi setup
Tanganyikan cichlids requiring softer or more neutral water

imageCandidates.notes: Male specimen photographed by Gerard Delany, July 2006. 536×391 px, 101 KB. Shows characteristic rusty-lavender coloration of a mature male. Attribution required; derivatives must use same or compatible license.

summary: The Rusty Cichlid is widely regarded as the gentlest mbuna in the hobby — an ideal introduction to Lake Malawi cichlids for intermediate keepers. Endemic to a handful of rocky islands in the lake’s southeastern arm, it reaches roughly 10 cm and displays striking rusty-orange to lavender-purple colouration. A maternal mouthbrooder, it breeds readily in captivity.
careNotes: Iodotropheus sprengerae is the least aggressive of the mbuna group, making it the recommended entry-point species for aquarists new to Malawi cichlids. Despite its comparatively gentle disposition it remains a cichlid and will establish territories among rockwork; adequate caves and visual barriers are essential. A tank of at least 90 cm footprint (approximately 120 L) suits a single-male group (1 male with 2–3 females); for multiple males or a species community, a minimum 120 cm footprint is recommended, which also reduces the risk of the fish being outcompeted in mixed mbuna setups. The species is sensitive to Malawi bloat — an intestinal condition linked to overfeeding protein-rich meaty foods — and the diet should be anchored in plant matter and spirulina. Bloodworms in particular should be limited to occasional treat quantities. Water quality must be kept high: Lake Malawi is exceptionally clear and oligotrophic, so nitrate accumulation is poorly tolerated; regular water changes (25–30% weekly) and efficient filtration are important. Hard, alkaline water (pH 7.5–8.8, gH 10–25) is non-negotiable; soft or acidic conditions cause rapid health decline. Males develop the characteristic lavender-purple sheen at maturity; juveniles and females retain the eponymous rusty-brown colouration. The species is Near Threatened in the wild (IUCN 2018) due to its extremely restricted endemic range.
breedingNotes: Polygamous maternal mouthbrooder that breeds readily in well-maintained aquaria. Males court females with lateral displays and flank shimmy behaviour around rocky territory. Females carry fertilised eggs and fry orally for approximately 21 days (3 weeks) before releasing free-swimming fry. Clutch sizes range from 5 to 60 fry, with larger females producing larger broods. Sexual maturity is reached at roughly 3.8 cm (1.5"). Holding females should ideally be moved to a separate container to avoid harassment, or the tank must provide dense rockwork so the female can hide undisturbed. Fry are released at a relatively large size and can accept finely crushed flake or nauplii from the start. No paternal care is provided. The species hybridises readily with related mbuna if mixed carelessly.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Iodotropheus sprengerae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 10 cm wild, ~7.5 cm captivity), temperature 25–29°C, pH 7.5–8.8, hardness 10–25°H, minimum tank dimensions (90×30×30 cm, 80 L for single-male group; 120 cm length for multiple males), diet (omnivore with vegetable foundation), temperament (least aggressive mbuna), compatible genera, native range (Boadzulu, Chinyankwazi, Chinyamwezi Islands, southern Lake Malawi), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 3-week hold, 5–60 fry, mature at 1.5").

additional.name: FishBase — Iodotropheus sprengerae
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (10.8 cm SL), temperature range (24–26°C), native range (Lake Malawi, Boadzulu and Chinyankwazi Islands, also Chinyamwezi Island), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Pseudocrenilabrinae), reproduction (female mouthbrooder), conservation status (Near Threatened). FishBase temperature range 24–26°C is narrower than SeriouslyFish’s 25–29°C; upper bound from SeriouslyFish retained as the more trade-applicable care range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Iodotropheus sprengerae
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum size (10.8 cm SL), sexual dichromatism (juveniles and females rusty-brown; mature males lavender-purple), endemic range restricted to islands and reefs in southeastern arm of Lake Malawi, omnivore diet (meaty foods linked to digestive problems), mouthbrooding reproduction, and “one of the least aggressive mbuna” characterisation. Named in honour of Kappy Sprenger; described by Oliver & Loiselle 1972.

additional.name: Wikimedia Commons — File:Iodotropheus sprengerae.jpg
additional.notes: Image candidate source. Photograph by Gerard Delany, CC BY-SA 2.5. Used to populate imageCandidates.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; females carry eggs and fry for ~21 days. Clutch 5–60 fry. Sexually mature at ~3.8 cm. No specialised equipment required beyond adequate rockwork and a 3:1 female-to-male ratio. Separating holding females reduces stress.
